We examine whether shared audit style increases the comparability of reported
earnings within a Big 4 auditor's clientele. We posit that two clients audited by
the same firm produce more comparable earnings because the firm applies a common
set of internal rules. We note, however, that client economics may dominate, so
the net effect is an empirical question. Consistent with our prediction, we find
evidence that same-auditor pairs are more comparable. The remainder of the paper
proceeds as follows.
